# Modern CLI Kit and TUIs

The commands in Parts I and II are the ones every Unix machine
has had for decades: `ls`, `cat`, `cd`, `grep`, `find`. A newer
generation of tools reimplements those same jobs with friendlier
defaults, color, and a few genuinely useful extras, and adds a
handful of full-screen programs for tasks the plain commands do
awkwardly. On the laptop in front of you they are a real
pleasure: `eza` shows a file's git status in the listing, `bat`
prints a script with line numbers and syntax highlighting,
`zoxide` jumps to a directory by a fragment of its name. This
chapter is a curated tour of the kit a researcher actually
benefits from, taught as ergonomic upgrades to commands you
already know.

It comes with an honest bill, and stating it plainly is the
point of the chapter. None of these tools is on the bare Linux
server you SSH into in Chapter 14. A fresh cloud VM or an HPC
login node has `ls` and `cat` and `grep`; it does not have
`eza` or `bat` or `zoxide`, and on a locked-down node you may
not be able to install them. The risk is not the tools; it is
the *habit*. Lean on them hard enough that your fingers forget
the originals, wire them into a script, and the first time you
land on a plain box the muscle memory fails. So the spine of
this chapter is: adopt what makes your laptop nicer, and keep it
a convenience layer that never becomes a dependency your work or
your reflexes rely on. You have already met two members of this
kit, `fd` [-@fdfind] and `ripgrep` [-@ripgrep], in Chapter 9; they carry the same tax,
and this chapter is the rest of the family plus the accounting.

A note on where the output comes from, and its shelf life. The
whole kit is a set of separately installed binaries that this
book's Linux sandbox cannot fetch (only `ripgrep` and `du` are
preinstalled there, both already covered), so, like the AI-CLI
chapter, this is a Mac-captured chapter. Every tool is
version-stamped "current as of 2026-07-06" and pinned to its
project repository (logged in `verification/chapter-16.md`); the
exact versions on the author's Mac are `eza` 0.23.4, `bat`
0.26.1, `delta` 0.19.2, `zoxide` 0.9.9, `fzf` 0.70.0, `lazygit`
0.62.2, `btop` 1.4.7, and `ncdu` 2.9.2, alongside the `fd`
10.4.2 and `ripgrep` 13.0.0 from Chapter 9. Every capture is run
with color and icons turned OFF, because color and Nerd-Font
icons are ANSI and Unicode that a transcript taken with color
off does not carry, and being blunt about that is part of the
honesty: each tool's own live color is a real part of its
payoff, and it is exactly what these color-off captures leave
out. The three pure
full-screen tools (`lazygit`, `btop`, `ncdu`) are version-
stamped but not run, because a full-screen program owns the
terminal and cannot be captured in a transcript, the same reason
Chapters 10 and 17 could not script an interactive session.
Where output would show the author's account or home path it
reads `[account]` instead, and `zoxide` was pointed at a
throwaway database so no real directory history appears.

## eza: `ls` with a git column

`eza` is a drop-in-nicer `ls` [-@ezacli]. It takes the same everyday flags
Chapter 5 taught, `-l` for a long listing, `-a` for hidden
files, and prints them with better defaults: human-readable
sizes without asking, and, on a project under version control,
a column showing each file's git status.

```bash
$ eza -l --git scripts
.rw-r--r--@ 7.9k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -- 00_make_data.py
.rw-r--r--@ 2.0k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -- 01_clean.py
.rw-r--r--@ 2.3k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -M 02_portfolio.py
.rw-r--r--@ 1.3k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -- 03_figure.py
.rw-r--r--@ 3.1k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -- 04_regression.R
.rw-r--r--@ 1.1k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -- bootstrap_renv.R
.rw-r--r--@ 3.7k [account]  6 Jul 22:09 -- check.py
```

Read it left to right and it is the `ls -l` layout from
Chapter 5 with the sizes already humanized: the permission
string (the `@` marks macOS extended attributes), the size, the
owner (masked here), the date, then the git column, then the
name. That git column is the reason to reach for `eza`. The two
characters encode staged and unstaged status, `--` for a clean
file and `-M` for one modified since the last commit, so
`02_portfolio.py` here has an uncommitted edit and the rest are
clean, all without a separate `git status`. `eza` can also draw
the directory as a tree (`eza --tree`), a live alternative to
the `ls -R` and hand-drawn maps of Chapter 6, though it uses
Unicode branch glyphs (the box-drawing characters this book
renders as ASCII), and it can prefix each file with a
type icon if you have a Nerd Font installed. The icons and the
color are the parts that do not survive print, and they are also
the parts you can most happily live without: the git column is
the substance.

## bat: `cat` that shows structure

`bat` is `cat` (Chapter 7) with the reading experience improved.
It prints a file with a line-number gutter, syntax highlighting
for the language, and, in a git repository, a change gutter
marking modified lines.

```bash
$ bat --style=numbers --decorations=always \
      --color=never --paging=never \
      --line-range=1:16 scripts/00_make_data.py
   1 """Generate a synthetic firm-month panel for the
   2 signal-sorted long-short example.
   3 
   4 All data in this project are SIMULATED. Nothing here is
   5 real CRSP, Compustat, or Bloomberg data. Generating from a
   6 seeded script is the whole point: licensed data cannot live
   7 in a repo, but a seed can, so anyone rebuilds the panel
   8 from this file alone. See PLAN.md Section 7.
   9 
  10 The data-generating process embeds one story: an accounting
  11 signal earns a long-short return that the five Fama-French
  12 factors do not span (the "mispricing" alpha). The
  13 econometrics is illustrative scaffolding; the book teaches
  14 the terminal, not asset pricing.
  15 
  16   R(i,t) = RF_t + beta_i . factors_t
```

The line numbers on the left are what the capture keeps; in your
terminal the Python keywords would be colored and, in a repo,
changed lines flagged in the gutter, and those are exactly the
parts this color-off capture cannot show. Two flags in that
command are worth understanding, because they are not how you
would type it day to day. Interactively you just run
`bat scripts/00_make_data.py`. The capture adds
`--decorations=always` because `bat` is deliberately terminal-
aware: when its output is not a terminal (piped into another
command, or redirected to a file as it is here), it quietly
falls back to plain `cat`-like text with no gutter, so that
`bat file | grep pattern` behaves exactly like `cat`. That
fallback is a feature, and it is also why `bat` is one of the
few tools here that is harmless in a pipeline: asked to feed
another program, it gets out of the way. `--color=never` and
`--paging=never` just fix the output for a static capture.

## zoxide and fzf: getting there faster

Two tools change how you *move* rather than how you *look*.
`zoxide` is a smarter `cd` (Chapter 5). It watches the
directories you visit and builds a ranked database, so instead
of typing a long path you type `z` and a fragment of the
directory's name and it jumps to the best match. The `z` command
itself comes from a shell hook you add to your startup file
(`eval "$(zoxide init zsh)"`), which is a dotfiles concern
Chapter 18 takes up; the underlying lookup is `zoxide query`,
and pointing it at a throwaway database seeded with just the
project shows the resolution directly:

```bash
$ zoxide query asset-pricing
/Users/[account]/projects/asset-pricing

$ zoxide query scripts
/Users/[account]/projects/asset-pricing/scripts
```

The catch is in the word "watches": `zoxide` only knows
directories you have already been to. On a machine you just
logged into, its database is empty and `z` finds nothing until
you have navigated around by hand for a while, which is the
portability tax in miniature.

`fzf` is a fuzzy finder: a general filter that takes any list on
standard input, opens a full-screen box you type into to narrow
it live, and prints the line you pick. The list can be files,
git branches, command history, anything a command emits. The
interactive box is the whole point and cannot be shown in a
transcript, but its matching logic is exposed non-interactively
with `--filter`, which takes a query and prints the matches with
no UI:

```bash
$ ls scripts | fzf --filter portfolio
02_portfolio.py
```

Interactively you would pipe a list into `fzf`, type `prt`, and
watch it narrow to `02_portfolio.py` as you go. Its most-used
form is a set of shell key bindings (a fuzzy history search on
`Ctrl-R`, a file picker on `Ctrl-T`) that, like the `zoxide`
hook, live in your startup files and belong to Chapter 18.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The tempting next step is to make these permanent by aliasing
the originals in your dotfiles: `alias ls=eza`, `alias cat=bat`,
`cd` replaced by `z`. Do that and your fingers stop knowing the
real commands, which is fine until you SSH into a bare server
(Chapter 14) where none of the replacements exist, and suddenly
`ls` behaves in an unfamiliar way (it is the real `ls` again),
`z` is an unknown command, and a script you wrote that calls
`bat` or `fd` fails outright. Two habits keep this bounded. Keep
your own reflexes on the portable commands, treating the kit as
a bonus rather than a replacement, and never let a convenience
tool into a script or a pipeline that has to run somewhere else.
Aliasing for interactive comfort is reasonable; Chapter 18 shows
how to do it so it degrades gracefully when the tool is absent.
:::

## delta: readable diffs (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

`delta` is a pager for diffs. It does not produce diffs; it
takes the output of `git diff` (or a plain `diff`) and re-renders
it with syntax highlighting, line numbers, and an optional
side-by-side layout, after you point git's pager at it in your
git config. Because that rendering is inherently colored, it is
the clearest case in the chapter of a payoff a printed page
cannot reproduce, so the honest thing to show is the portable
input `delta` consumes: the plain `git diff` it pages. This is
the one-line edit from the review example in Chapter 17, a
long-short sort quietly changed from deciles to quintiles:

```bash
$ git diff scripts/02_portfolio.py
diff --git a/scripts/02_portfolio.py b/scripts/02_portfolio.py
index ba00349..34d369a 100644
--- a/scripts/02_portfolio.py
+++ b/scripts/02_portfolio.py
@@ -20,7 +20,7 @@ import polars as pl
 CLEAN = Path("data/clean/analysis.parquet")
 FACTORS = Path("data/raw/factors.csv")
 OUT = Path("data/clean/portfolio.parquet")
-N_PORT = 10  # deciles
+N_PORT = 5  # quintiles
 
 
 def main() -> None:
```

Piped through `delta`, that same diff gains colored additions
and deletions, the file name in a header, and line numbers down
the side, which on a large diff genuinely helps you find the
change that matters. What it does not change is the information:
the plain diff above already tells you, exactly, that one line
moved from 10 to 5. `delta` makes a diff pleasanter to read; the
diff itself, which is the thing your review depends on, is what
git gives you on any machine, styled or not.

## The TUIs: lazygit, btop, and ncdu (ADVANCED) {.tier-advanced}

A TUI, a terminal user interface, is a full-screen program that
takes over the whole terminal, redraws as you press keys, and
hands the screen back only when you quit. That design is why the
three tools here are described rather than shown: a TUI produces
no line-by-line output to capture, it owns the display, so, like
an editor or an interactive agent session (Chapters 10 and 17),
it cannot honestly appear as a transcript. Their versions are
real, captured on the Mac; the walkthroughs are prose.

`lazygit` (0.62.2, current as of 2026-07-06) is a full-screen
front end for git: it shows your status, staged and unstaged
changes, branches, and log in panels you drive by keystroke, so
you stage a hunk or rewrite a branch without typing the git
commands. It complements the command-line git the companion Git
book teaches; some people find it faster for interactive work,
and nothing in this book requires it.

`btop` (1.4.7) is a resource monitor, a friendlier `top`: live
CPU, memory, network, and per-process usage in a color
dashboard. It is genuinely handy for answering "what is pinning
this machine right now," on your laptop or on a server you can
run it on.

`ncdu` (2.9.2) is the interactive, navigable `du` promised in
Chapter 14: NCurses Disk Usage. It scans a directory tree once,
then lets you walk it sorted by size to find what is filling a
disk, which is precisely the "why is my quota full" task on a
shared server. Of the tools in this chapter, `ncdu` is the one
you are most likely to actually want on a server, and, as
Chapter 14 noted, the one you can sometimes get there through a
module system or a userspace install.

## The portability tax

Everything above earns its place on your laptop and loses it on
a bare server, so it is worth naming precisely what breaks and
what does not. The table maps each tool to the command it
upgrades, whether you can count on it on a plain server, and
whether it belongs in a script, which doubles as your fallback
list: when the modern tool is missing, the command in the
second column is what you reach for.

| Tool | Upgrades | On a bare server | Belongs in a script |
|---|---|---|---|
| eza | `ls -l` | no | no, use `ls` |
| bat | `cat` | no | no, use `cat` |
| zoxide | `cd` | no | no, use `cd` |
| fzf | a picker | no | only via `--filter` |
| delta | `git diff` | no | no, use `git diff` |
| fd | `find` | rarely | no, use `find` |
| ripgrep | `grep -r` | sometimes | only if installed |
| lazygit | `git` (TUI) | no | no |
| btop | `top` (TUI) | no | no |
| ncdu | `du` (TUI) | sometimes | no |

The "on a bare server" column is the whole tax in one place:
almost every answer is no. What you can rely on anywhere is the
first two Parts of this book, the portable commands that ship
with the operating system. The kit sits on top of them for
comfort; it never sits underneath your work.

::: {.callout-note .divergence title="DIVERGENCE"}
The install story diverges hard, and that divergence is part of
the tax. On your Mac it is `brew install`. On a Linux box you
control it is `apt`/`dnf` or `cargo install`, and the binary
names differ: to avoid clashing with packages that already own
the short name, Debian and Ubuntu install `fd` from the
`fd-find` package as `fdfind`, and `bat` as `batcat` (each
tool's README suggests you symlink it back in `~/.local/bin`).
The exact name depends on the distribution and release, so a
script or alias that calls `fd` or `bat` by name can fail on a
Linux box where the tool is in fact installed, just under a
different name. On a locked-down
login node with no sudo and no `cargo`, there is no install
story at all: you get the coreutils the OS shipped and nothing
more. Check a binary's real name on the box with `command -v`
(Chapter 2) before relying on it. All current as of 2026-07-06.
:::

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The reproducible pipeline depends on none of this kit. The
Makefile of Chapter 12 builds the study with `uv run python`,
`Rscript`, and `quarto`, and `make check` re-derives the locked
hash and alpha with a Python script; nowhere does it call `eza`,
`bat`, `delta`, or `zoxide`. Those are for the human reading the
screen, never part of the build graph, and that boundary is the
point: a convenience tool wired into a pipeline becomes a
dependency that breaks the moment the pipeline runs where the
tool is absent. The fail-loudly scripting of Chapter 11 and the
Makefile of Chapter 12 stay on the tools the build actually
needs; the kit stays at your prompt. What those build tools
themselves require, `uv`, R, and Quarto, is a separate
portability question, handled by Chapters 13 and 14.
:::

::: {.callout-tip .recovery title="RECOVERY"}
When you land on a bare server and your reflexes misfire, `eza`
and `bat` and `z` all "command not found," the recovery is not a
fix but a fallback, and you already have it. Everything the kit
does, the portable commands from Parts I and II do too:
`ls -l` for the listing, `cat` or `less` to read a file, `cd`
to move, `grep` and `find` to search. They are always there,
which is the entire reason this book taught them first. Nothing
is lost by the tools being absent, because they were only ever a
comfort layer over commands that never left.
:::

Honest cost and benefit, then. On a daily-driver laptop this kit
is a real ergonomic win, and adopting the pieces you like is a
reasonable choice; `eza`'s git column and `ncdu`'s disk walk in
particular pay for themselves quickly. The cost is not on the
laptop. It is the portability tax, paid in two currencies:
habits that strand you on a plain server, and any dependency you
let creep into work that has to run somewhere else. Spend on the
comfort, refuse the dependency, and the kit is all upside.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

These use the asset-pricing project from Chapter 6 and, in the
last two, the remote box from Chapter 14.

1. Install one or two of the kit with `brew install` (say `eza`
   and `bat`), then run `eza -l --git scripts` and
   `bat scripts/02_portfolio.py` in the project. Note what each
   adds over the `ls -l` and `cat` you already know, and which
   of those additions is color that a script or a pipe would not
   see.

2. Let `zoxide` learn a directory. With the `zoxide` shell hook
   active, `cd` into the project once the long way, then from
   your home directory run `z asset-pricing` and confirm it
   jumps there. Then explain, in one line, why the same command
   finds nothing on a machine you just logged into.

3. See what `delta` adds and what it does not. Configure git to
   page through `delta`, make a one-line edit to a script, and
   run `git diff`; then turn `delta` off and run the same
   `git diff`. Write down what changed in the *presentation* and
   confirm the actual change reported is identical either way.

4. Feel the tax. SSH into the bare server from Chapter 14 and
   try `eza`, `bat`, and `z`. Watch them fail with "command not
   found," then do the same jobs with `ls -l`, `cat`, and `cd`.
   Name the one habit that would have slowed you down most if
   you had aliased it away.

5. Audit your pipeline. In the project, `grep` the `Makefile`
   and `scripts/check.py` for `eza`, `bat`, `fd`, and `rg`.
   Confirm the build graph calls none of them: the modern kit is
   nowhere in the path that rebuilds the study, which is exactly
   what keeps the study rebuildable without it.
